attributes.stem:
An eight-month-old girl presents to the Emergency Department with her parents and is reviewed by the duty registrar. She is crying and not moving her left arm. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her immunisations are up to date.

On examination, she appears distressed. Her vital signs are within normal limits. There is a visible deformity of the left upper arm and bruising over the left side of her chest. Her parents are unable to provide a clear history of how the injury occurred. A radiograph reveals a spiral fracture of the left humerus.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Request security to remove the parents
attributes.explanation:
This case presents several concerning features suggestive of non-accidental injury: a spiral fracture of the humerus, bruising in a non-mobile infant, and no clear history of trauma. The first step in managing suspected child abuse is to discuss with senior consultant to develop an appropriate management plan.

Contact child protection services will likely be necessary, but this should occur after discussion with senior staff to ensure appropriate documentation and handover of information. While healthcare professionals have a mandatory duty to report suspected abuse, the process should be coordinated.

Request security to remove the parents would be inappropriate and potentially dangerous. Unless there is immediate threat to the child's safety, maintaining a therapeutic relationship with the family is important while investigations proceed.

Arrange urgent social work review may be helpful but should not delay senior medical review and mandatory reporting. Social work can provide valuable support but does not replace the clinician's duty to report suspected abuse.

Arrange skeletal survey may be indicated to identify occult injuries but should not delay the reporting process. This investigation should be arranged after discussion with senior staff and child protection services.

The key principles in managing suspected child abuse are to ensure the immediate safety of the child, maintain thorough documentation, and follow local mandatory reporting procedures. A multi-disciplinary approach involving senior medical staff, child protection services, and social work is essential.
learningPoints: Bruising in non-mobile infants and spiral fractures without clear mechanism should raise suspicion of non-accidental injury.
Discussion with a senior consultant is the first step in managing suspected non-accidental injury.
Healthcare professionals have a mandatory duty to report suspected child abuse.
references: Ward M, Ornstein A, Niec A, Murray L. The medical assessment of bruising in suspected child maltreatment cases: A clinical perspective. Paediatr Child Health. 2013; 18(8):433-437. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24426797/

Mandelstam S, Cook D, Fitzgerald M, Ditchfield M, Carty H. Complementary use of radiological skeletal survey and bone scintigraphy in detection of bony injuries in suspected child abuse. Arch Dis Child. 2003; 88(5):387-390. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12716705/
Bird S. Child abuse Mandatory reporting requirements. Aust Fam Phys. 2011; 40(11):921-926. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/4a851b5d-ed2e-40d2-a046-5a8d0e1fe8f1/Child-abuse.aspx
